entry.title: Re: Integration of SAP Ariba Buying Procurement Expansion...
entry.content:
Good day @AFROUJA 

Thank you for your question.

Your 3 questions are mostly about the cross-module workflow inside SAP Ariba rather than a pure Public Cloud process. Based on SAP, the standard pattern is:

  • Buying creates or holds the operational demand.
  • Sourcing is used when the demand is sent into a sourcing request or sourcing event.
  • Contract is then used when the awarded result is turned into a contract request and then a contract.
  • The contract pricing is typically used for future contracted buying and release-order behaviour, rather than retroactively rewriting the original submitted purchase request.

Here are references for more guidance.

https://help.sap.com/doc/ca9f0cb368414eb5a75c589bb89ce378/2602/en-US/SelfServiceParameters_1.pdf

https://help.sap.com/doc/058525a5bf7447bab1f5f52bd88eec25/2511/en-US/ContractCompliance.pdf

 

Question 1: Sourcing Integration: How does the Procurement Expansion Pack integrate with SAP Ariba Sourcing to initiate RFI and RFP processes from purchase requests created in SAP Ariba Buying?

Answer: You can create a sourcing request from a purchase requisition that is created in SAP Ariba Buying or SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing when you use the optional Quick Sourcing for Requisition feature. In practice, this means the purchase request in Buying becomes the trigger document, and the sourcing team continues the process in SAP Ariba Sourcing by creating the sourcing request and then the event.

So, for your first question, the standard workflow is not “Buying directly runs RFI/RFP itself”. Instead, the demand starts in Buying, then a sourcing request is created from that requisition, and the actual RFI/RFP event is handled in SAP Ariba Sourcing.

3174723 - How does the quick sourcing from requisition complete flow works? - SAP for Me

 

Question 2: Contracts Integration: How does the solution connect with SAP Ariba Contracts for:

  • Contract creation linked to purchase requests?
  • Price updates flowing back to the original purchase request?

Answer: For Contracts, buyers can create a contract request from a sourcing award when the relevant site parameter (Application.ACM.CreateContractRequestFromAwardedEvent.Enabled) is enabled. So, when you set this to Yes, buyers can create a contract request from the awarded sourcing event. So, the normal path is:

  1. Demand begins in Buying,
  2. Sourcing request/event is process in Sourcing,
  3. Supplier is awarded,
  4. A contract request is created from that sourcing award,
  5. After approval, the contract is created.

For your sub question on Contract creation linked to purchase requests, the link is usually indirect, not “purchase request becomes contract immediately”. The requisition can trigger sourcing, and the sourcing award can trigger a contract request. I am not aware of 81Y module flow saying that a normal purchase request in Buying is directly transformed into a contract workspace without the sourcing or contract-request step in between.

Regarding Price updates back to the original purchase request, I would not describe the standard behaviour as “contract price updates flow back to the original purchase request”. Note that release order contract is used to consume funds from the contract, and when users select items covered by a contract, the contract is attached to the requisition item and the contract price is transparently applied to them. See Managing Contract Compliance for more details.

So, the more accurate standard interpretation is:

  • The sourcing award can lead to a contract request,
  • The contract is created and approved,
  • The contract then governs future contracted requisitions, release orders, and downstream purchasing, but not a standard workflow where the original purchase request is reopened and repriced from the contract workspace as part of this 81Y process.

If your business expectation is that the awarded or contracted price should automatically overwrite the original purchase request document, the standard model is that contract pricing is used when users buy against the contract afterward.

 

Question 3: End-to-End Workflow: What is the typical data flow and process sequence across these three modules?

Anwer: Standard:

  • Create a sourcing request from a requisition in SAP Ariba Buying.
  • Run the event in SAP Ariba Sourcing.
  • Create a contract request from the sourcing award, if you have enabled the site parameter.
  • Use the resulting contract for contracted buying and release-order pricing afterward.

I wouldn’t opt for the following as a standard:

  • Direct PR-to-contract creation in this exact 81Y cross-module flow without the sourcing-award/contract-request step.
  • Automatic repricing of the already-created original purchase request from the contract workspace.

 

To summarise:

For 81Y, the standard cross-module flow is:

Buying requisition à Sourcing request/RFI/RFP in Sourcing à Award à Contract request from the award à Contract à Future contracted buying against that contract.

 

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

SAP GenAI was used to help generate this content

entry.title: Re: In SAP Public cloud, check consistency before MRP run...
entry.content:
Good day @SANDY13973 

Thank you for your question.

If your gaol is to make sure your MRP output is only accepted when BOM, routing, and production version are correct, then the clean approach is for you to:

  • Use Process Production Versions as the primary pre-check

  • Use Maintain Bill of Material/Explode BOM to confirm BOM

  • Confirm routing in Manage Routings

  • Should you have any missing valid version, or you must repair a version, you can use Schedule Mass Creation of Production Version
  • Then use MRP execution and coverage review as your operational acceptance step after the run.

 

There is no single Public Cloud acceptance gate before MRP output, but the standard control object is the production version, supported by BOM and routing validation before MRP and exception-based review after MRP.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

In order to decide which attributes should become Competencies & which attributes should become Skills, we need to understand the differences in functionalities across other SuccessFactors modules. I do understand the conceptual difference between both, and that Competencies hold "Behaviors" but Skills don't, although their use-case also seems to differ (e.g. in recruitment, you can only use skills for matching, not competencies). 

Is there an overview available, or any information to support the decision in what situation we only use skills, where we only use competencies and where we can use both?

entry.title: Re: Integration Suite Authentication
entry.content:
Thanks for the detailed context, @dr_vera_r.  
 
The behavior you’re seeing (HTTP 401 Unauthorized) is consistent with calling an SAP Cloud Integration (Process Integration Runtime) endpoint without the required inbound authentication.

What “ESBMessaging.send / MessagingSend” is used for

For inbound calls into SAP Cloud Integration, SAP’s documentation describes two common authentication options:
  • Basic authentication: use a user that has either MessagingSend (Cloud Foundry) or ESBMessaging.send (Neo) assigned in the SAP BTP cockpit. [help.sap.com]
  • OAuth2 Client Credentials (Cloud Foundry): use client ID, client secret, and token URL from the service key of your SAP Cloud Integration service instance. [help.sap.com]
So, even if your target API is secured via API‑Key, the inbound endpoint of the iFlow still expects one of the supported inbound auth mechanisms (Basic or OAuth2 client credentials), otherwise you’ll get 401. [help.sap.com]

How to get the missing Token URL / Client ID / Client Secret

The same SAP Help Portal guidance explains where those OAuth2 values come from:
Retrieve them from the service key of the SAP Cloud Integration service instance in the relevant subaccount (BTP cockpit → Instances and Subscriptions → Instances → select the Cloud Integration instance → Credentials → service key). [help.sap.com]
If the JSON you see for your service key doesn’t contain clientid, clientsecret, and a token URL, double‑check that:
  1. you are looking at the service key of the SAP Cloud Integration service instance (not a different instance), and
  2. it’s the service key under the subaccount where the Cloud Integration tenant is running. [help.sap.com]

Where OAuth2 “credentials” are maintained in the tenant (outbound use cases)

Separately (for outbound calls from an iFlow to an external system), SAP documents maintaining credentials in the tenant via:
Monitor Integrations and APIs → Manage Security → Security Material → Create → OAuth2 Client Credentials [help.sap.com]
And for third‑party providers in general, the guidance is to obtain the provider credentials and then create the corresponding Security Material type in Cloud Integration. [help.sap.com]
Note: This “Security Material” setup is typically relevant when the iFlow needs to call your API (outbound). Your current 401 looks more like an inbound authentication issue to the Cloud Integration endpoint. [help.sap.com]

About “disabling auth on the SAP side”

I didn’t find SAP Help Portal guidance that suggests running Cloud Integration inbound endpoints with no authentication. The documented patterns for calling the endpoint use Basic (MessagingSend/ESBMessaging.send) or OAuth2 client credentials.
So the practical way forward is usually to pick the simpler inbound option (often Basic for quick testing) and then move to OAuth2 client credentials if required by your setup. [help.sap.com]

Sources

  • Create Webhook (includes Basic vs OAuth2 client credentials and MessagingSend/ESBMessaging.send, plus where to find clientId/clientSecret/tokenUrl in service keys)
  • Establish Data Provider Integration… (example of creating OAuth2 Client Credentials under Security Material in Cloud Integration)
  • Establish Data Provider Integration with Third‑Party Data Provider (general guidance: create Security Material matching the provider)
Note: This response was prepared with AI‑powered assistance like SAP Joule for Consultants (J4C), see https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/ai-assistant/sap-consulting-capability.html. It has been reviewed by an SAP subject matter expert. Please validate against the linked official documentation for your specific release.
You may want to consider getting SAP Joule for Consultants for yourself. Currently, SAP has a promotional offering for Silver, Gold and Platinum partners available in the Partner Benefits Catalog: https://partnerbenefitscatalog.sap.com/Partner-Benefits-Catalog/Customer-Engagement-%26-Post-Sales/Joule-for-Consultants-Initiative-2026/p/1002479.
 
If this answer helped you, please mark the response accordingly. Thank you.

entry.title: 🚀 AI & Claude Code Experiment (Part 2): Building an SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget
entry.content:
Introduction and Context

In this blog, I explore how to bring external visualization libraries into SAP Analytics Cloud by building a custom widget based on an Apache ECharts example using Claude Code. Starting from an existing ECharts sample, I walk through how it can be adapted for SAC and turned into a working custom widget.

If you have read my previous blog on building an SAC widget add-on using Claude Code (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/building-an-sap-analytics-cloud-widget-add-on-with-claude-code/ba-p/14366149), this is a natural next step. While widget add-ons focus on extending existing capabilities, custom widgets give you much more flexibility but also introduce additional complexity, especially when integrating external libraries and handling rendering and data flows.

In this scenario, Apache ECharts is used as the starting point for the visualization. However, the goal is not to simply copy an existing ECharts example into SAP Analytics Cloud. Instead, the objective is to take the idea behind the visualization and turn it into a working SAC custom widget.

This means that ECharts is reused as the charting engine, while the surrounding widget logic is implemented specifically for SAP Analytics Cloud. The SAC integration, including the custom widget structure, lifecycle handling, data binding, and the transformation of SAC data into the format required by ECharts, is built for this scenario.

In other words, the visualization concept comes from ECharts, but the SAC custom widget itself is not just reused. It is adapted and implemented so that it works properly within the SAP Analytics Cloud environment.

So while ECharts is responsible for rendering the chart itself, much of the actual implementation effort lies in building the SAC-specific integration around it. This is also what makes custom widgets more flexible, but at the same time more complex than widget add-ons.

If you are interested in topics like SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP Business Data Cloud, and practical AI-assisted development scenarios like this one, feel free to join my LinkedIn community, where I regularly share insights, experiments, and hands-on content: https://www.linkedin.com/groups/13112863/

Why Apache ECharts?

After setting the context, the next step is to choose a suitable visualization library. In principle, there are many options available, and Apache ECharts ( https://echarts.apache.org/en/index.html ) is just one of them. I decided to use Apache ECharts because I have come across several interesting and visually appealing chart examples in the past. It offers a wide range of chart types and interaction patterns, which makes it a good candidate when looking for more advanced visualizations beyond standard capabilities. 

For this blog, the goal is not to explore ECharts in detail, but rather to use one of its examples as a starting point and adapt it for SAP Analytics Cloud. The idea is to take an existing visualization and bring it into the SAC context by building a custom widget around it.

What is Apache ECharts?

Apache ECharts is an open-source JavaScript visualization library developed by the Apache Software Foundation (https://echarts.apache.org/en/index.html). It is designed for building interactive and highly customizable charts that can run in modern web browsers. The library provides a wide range of chart types, including line charts, bar charts, scatter plots, and more advanced visualizations such as heatmaps, radar charts, and animated data-driven charts. One of its key strengths is flexibility, as charts are configured using a structured option object that defines axes, series, styling, and interactions. ECharts is widely used for creating rich, interactive dashboards and supports features such as animations, tooltips, zooming, and dynamic updates. This makes it a strong candidate when looking for visualization capabilities that go beyond standard charting options.

Selected Example and Target Visualization

To make this more concrete, I selected one specific example from the Apache ECharts gallery, the Line Race Chart (https://echarts.apache.org/examples/en/editor.html?c=line-race), which stood out due to its dynamic and animated behavior.

The chart visualizes data in a more engaging way compared to standard charts, with smooth transitions and a strong focus on how values evolve over time. This makes it a good candidate to demonstrate what can be achieved when combining external visualization libraries with SAP Analytics Cloud.

The goal is to take this example as inspiration and recreate it as a custom widget within SAC, adapting it so that it works with SAC data and fits into the SAC story environment.

Before going into the implementation details, the following short preview shows what the final result will look like in SAC.

Start building the Custom Widget with Claude Code

Before starting with the implementation, a quick note on the setup. I am working on a Windows environment and use a dedicated project folder for this experiment.

In my case, the folder is C:\SAPDevelopSAC, and I start Claude Code directly from within this directory. All relevant files for the custom widget will be stored there, which keeps the project setup simple and organized.

As shown below, I navigate to the folder using PowerShell and start Claude Code directly from there.

After starting Claude Code, I can begin by describing what I want to build. 
Just an important note before we start.

So fefore continuing with the actual widget implementation, one additional detail is worth mentioning. In this experiment, I am also using the Superpowers plugin for Claude Code:
https://claude.com/plugins/superpowers

This plugin can be installed directly in Claude Code before starting the actual work. In my case, I used the following command:

claude plugin install superpowers@claude-plugins-official

Alternatively, the same installation can also be triggered from within Claude Code using:

/plugin install superpowers@claude-plugins-official

This is a simple preparation step before starting the implementation itself. Once installed, the plugin helps Claude Code work in a more structured way by breaking tasks into smaller and more transparent steps. It is not required, but in practice it helps a lot.

And now let's continue with our main task.

In this case, I tell Claude that I want to create a custom widget for SAP Analytics Cloud. To make the request more concrete, I also provide a reference implementation from GitHub so that Claude can understand the expected structure and setup of a SAC custom widget.

The example I am using is available here:
https://github.com/SAP-samples/analytics-cloud-datasphere-community-content/tree/main/SAC_Custom_Widgets/Sunbrust%20Chart%20with%20Styling%20Panel

By providing this reference, Claude Code can align its output with the typical structure of SAC custom widgets, which helps to avoid unnecessary iterations later on.

As shown below, I combine both the goal and the reference in my initial prompt.

Without providing any additional instructions, I wanted to see how far Claude Code could get on its own.

After submitting the initial prompt, Claude immediately starts analyzing the project context and attempts to fetch the referenced GitHub repository to understand the structure of the example.

At this point, Claude asks for permission to access the external resource. This is an important step, as it ensures that external content is only accessed with explicit approval.

As shown below, I allow Claude to fetch the GitHub content so it can continue analyzing the example.

In my setup, I configured Claude Code to always ask for permission before accessing external resources. This behavior can be adjusted, but I prefer to keep full control over what is being accessed.

As a result, Claude asks for confirmation before fetching content from GitHub, in this case from raw.githubusercontent.com, where the actual files of the repository are hosted.

After confirming the request, Claude proceeds with analyzing the example and continues working with the retrieved project structure.

What is particularly interesting at this point is how much Claude Code is able to derive from just a single prompt and the provided reference.

Without any further instructions, Claude explores the repository, fetches the relevant files, and identifies the core structure of a SAC custom widget. It correctly recognizes key components such as the index.json manifest, the main.js implementation, and the use of web components, Shadow DOM, and SAC lifecycle hooks.

This shows that Claude is not just retrieving content, but actively interpreting the structure and translating it into meaningful context for the task at hand.

Based on this understanding, Claude then starts asking more targeted questions about the intended design and functionality, which helps to move from a general idea to a more concrete implementation.

At this point, I provide Claude Code with a more concrete direction.

Instead of keeping the request generic, I now specify that I want to build a custom widget based on a specific Apache ECharts example, the Line Race Chart.

The goal is to take this visualization and adapt it so that it works as a custom widget within SAP Analytics Cloud, including proper data binding and integration into the SAC lifecycle.

To achieve this, I use the following prompt:

I want to build a custom widget for SAP Analytics Cloud based on a specific Apache ECharts example.

Please use the following chart as the reference and adapt it into a SAC custom widget:  
https://echarts.apache.org/examples/en/editor.html?c=line-race

The widget should:
- follow the standard SAC custom widget structure you identified earlier  
- use ECharts as the rendering library  
- be able to consume data from SAC (data binding)  
- transform the SAC data into the format required by the ECharts line race chart  

Start by creating the basic widget structure and integrating the ECharts example. We can refine styling and additional features later.

This prompt builds on the context Claude has already gathered and provides a clear target for the implementation.
To use the prompt, simply copy it from this blog post and paste it into Claude Code.

On Windows, this can be done easily by right-clicking inside the Claude Code interface, which will automatically paste the copied prompt into the input field.

As shown below, Claude recognizes the pasted content and prepares it for execution. After that, simply press Enter to execute the prompt.

At this point, Claude also starts to work in a more structured way, as I am using the Superpowers skill. This helps Claude break the task down into smaller steps, making the overall process more transparent and easier to follow.

Instead of jumping straight into implementation, Claude first analyzes the request, explores the project context, and outlines the next actions before continuing.

After confirming the first request with Yes, Claude continues fetching additional resources required for the chart. Since the ECharts example consists of multiple assets, you may be prompted again. At this point, instead of selecting Yes again, I chose:

“Yes, and don't ask again for echarts.apache.org”

This prevents Claude Code from repeatedly asking for permission when accessing the same domain. Especially when working with external examples or libraries, this makes the process much smoother and avoids unnecessary interruptions.

Next, Claude starts fetching the actual Apache ECharts example that we referenced earlier. In this case, it retrieves the line race chart source directly from the ECharts repository.

As before, Claude asks for permission to access the external resource. Since this content comes from a trusted source and is required for the implementation, I selected:

“Yes, and don't ask again for raw.githubusercontent.com”

This ensures that Claude can continue working without repeated interruptions when accessing related files from the same domain.

At this point, you can clearly see how Claude connects all pieces together. It analyzes the ECharts example, inspects the existing widget structure, and prepares the next steps for building the custom widget.

At this stage, Claude suggests creating visual mockups in a browser to support the design process. While this can be helpful, it is not always necessary and may consume additional tokens.

In my case, I decided to keep things simple and focus directly on the implementation. Instead of using the visual companion, I guided Claude with a short instruction:

“No, just go ahead and work. If you need guidance, ask me here.”

This keeps the workflow lightweight and efficient. Claude continues with the task and only asks for input when it is actually needed.

After that, Claude decides to perform an additional web search to better understand the ECharts line race implementation, including how the animation and data updates are handled.

Again, Claude asks for permission before executing the request. To avoid repeated prompts during similar operations, I selected:

“Yes, and don't ask again for Web Search commands in this project”

This allows Claude to continue researching in the background without interrupting the workflow.

At this point, it becomes clear that Claude is not just following instructions, but actively trying to understand the underlying logic of the chart, including how data is updated over time and how the animation is driven.

Since the process was taking longer than expected, I used the /btw command to ask Claude Code what was causing the delay.

Claude responded that it had run into a 404 error while trying to access the example source in the expected way. Instead of waiting longer, I asked whether it would help to download the example manually.

Claude then suggested a practical workaround: open the Apache ECharts example in the browser, copy the full JavaScript code from the editor, and save it locally in the project folder. This would allow Claude to read the example directly from the local file instead of relying on the failed remote access.

This is a good example of how the interaction remains flexible. Even when an automated step does not work as expected, Claude can still suggest an alternative path to keep the implementation moving forward.

As shown above, you can use the Download button directly in the ECharts editor. This gives you the full example, including the HTML structure, script references, and chart configuration.

I then saved the downloaded file in my project folder:

C:\SAPDevelopSAC

 


This approach has a clear advantage: instead of working with isolated code snippets, Claude can now analyze the complete example, making it easier to understand how everything fits together (though in another earlier test Claude Code was able to access the online page))

After saving the file, I informed Claude that the example is available locally so it can continue working with it directly.

After downloading the example and saving it locally, I pointed Claude Code to the file in the project folder.

Claude then analyzed the example in more detail and extracted several important implementation aspects, including the animation behavior, the data structure used by the chart, and the way ECharts transforms and encodes the data internally.

Based on this analysis, Claude proposed a concrete SAC widget design. Instead of jumping straight into coding, it first outlined the expected file structure, the required data bindings, the transformation logic from SAC data to the ECharts format, and the initial widget properties.

This is one of the most interesting parts of the process. Claude Code is not just reading the example, it is already translating the original ECharts implementation into a SAC-specific design proposal and asks for confirmation before moving on to the actual implementation.

 After reviewing the proposed design, everything looked consistent and aligned with what I had in mind. Instead of making further adjustments at this stage, I decided to move forward.

I simply responded with:

“All good. Please continue!”

With this confirmation, Claude transitions from the design phase into the implementation phase. At this point, it starts generating the actual widget structure and code based on the previously defined architecture.

This step highlights an important aspect of the workflow: you stay in control of the direction, while Claude handles the heavy lifting once the approach is clear.

After I confirmed the overall design, Claude Code generated an implementation plan and prepared the next execution steps.

Once that looked fine, I simply replied with:

“Ok start”

From there, Claude switched into execution mode. It began creating the project structure, generated the first widget files, and started with the initial index.json manifest for the SAC custom widget.

What is interesting here is that Claude not only writes code, but also validates parts of the setup while progressing through the implementation steps. This makes the process easier to follow and gives additional confidence that the generated files are structurally consistent.


After confirming the execution step, I approved the remaining prompts with “Yes”. From that point on, Claude Code continued autonomously and started executing the plan step by step. 

You can clearly see how the predefined tasks are processed in sequence:

  • Creating the widget folder and index.json
  • Writing the main implementation file (main.js)
  • Running quick validation checks (for example, syntax validation)
  • Continuing with the remaining tasks like styling and test setup

What stands out here is the structured execution. Claude does not generate everything at once, instead it works through the plan like a pipeline, validating each step before moving on.

This makes the whole process feel less like “AI guessing code” and more like a controlled build process with clear milestones.

After creating all required files like:

- index.json
- main.js
- styling.js
- test-harness.html

Claude automatically prepares a ZIP archive using a PowerShell command:

Compress-Archive -Path ... -DestinationPath line-race-widget.zip

Before executing it, Claude asks for confirmation, since this step interacts directly with the local system.

Once approved, the widget is packaged and ready to be uploaded into SAP Analytics Cloud.
The output was not just code fragments, but a complete, structured result:

  • A valid widget manifest (index.json)
  • The main implementation (main.js)
  • A styling configuration (styling.js)
  • A local test harness for quick browser validation
  • And finally, a ready-to-use ZIP file for SAP Analytics Cloud

Claude even confirmed that all files passed basic checks like JSON validity and JavaScript syntax.

What makes this moment important is that we are no longer in a prototyping phase.
We now have a deployable artifact that can be directly uploaded into SAP Analytics Cloud.

The folder with the files is available:

 And also the zip file:

We open SAP Analytics Cloud and open the  

In SAP Analytics Cloud, navigate to:

Stories → Custom Widgets

From there, click the “+” button to upload a new widget. Select the generated ZIP file (line-race-widget.zip) and import it into your tenant.

Once uploaded, the widget becomes available in your environment and can be used inside a story or analytic application.

To use it, open a story and navigate to:

Insert → Custom Widgets

There, the newly uploaded widget appears in the list. In my case, it shows up as:

Line Race Chart v1.0.0

After selecting it, the widget can be placed directly onto the canvas like any other component.

At this point, the full journey is complete:

  • From defining the idea
  • To guiding Claude Code through the implementation
  • To generating a fully packaged widget
  • And finally integrating it into SAP Analytics Cloud

Initially, the widget shows a placeholder message:

“No data — bind Time, Category and a Measure.”

To resolve this, the required data bindings need to be configured in the Builder panel:

  • Time (X Axis) → first dimension
  • Category (Lines) → second dimension
  • Measure (Y Axis) → numeric value

Optionally, filters can be applied. In my case, I added a filter on the year to control the visible time range.
At this point, the widget becomes fully functional and renders the animated line race chart based on real data.

After assigning:

  • Time → Calendar Month
  • Category → Customer Segment
  • Measure → Order Value
  • Filter → Year

the chart immediately starts rendering and animating.

This is the moment where everything connects, the data model from SAP Analytics Cloud, the custom widget structure, and the ECharts visualization logic generated earlier.

What started as a prompt is now a fully working, animated visualization running inside SAC.

It is worth noting that this setup could be extended further. For example, I could have instructed Claude Code to generate an additional selection control for categories. However, for this scenario, using the built-in SAC data binding is completely sufficient.

At this point, the widget is fully functional and integrated into SAP Analytics Cloud.

Of course, there is still room for further enhancements. For example:

  • Adding a play or replay button for the animation
  • Customizing colors or themes
  • Extending the widget with additional controls or interactions

All of these could be implemented by continuing the collaboration with Claude Code.

However, for this experiment, I decided to stop here. The goal was to validate the end-to-end workflow, from idea to a working custom widget in SAP Analytics Cloud, and that goal has clearly been achieved.

So I wrapped things up with a simple message:

“Everything works fine. Thank you very much.”

And that perfectly captures the experience.

Final Summary & Takeaways

What we built here is more than just a chart.

Starting from a simple idea, we used Claude Code to design, implement, validate, and package a fully functional custom widget for SAP Analytics Cloud. The entire process, from prompt to deployment, happened in a structured and guided way.

This includes:

  • Translating a visualization idea into a concrete technical design
  • Generating a complete widget architecture with multiple files
  • Implementing logic, styling, and data transformation
  • Automatically validating and packaging the result
  • Deploying and running it inside a real SAC environment with live data

Why this is powerful

What makes this especially interesting is not the chart itself, but the way it was created.

This workflow fundamentally changes how custom development can be approached:

  • From manual coding → to guided orchestration
    You are no longer writing every line of code yourself. Instead, you define intent and guide the process.
  • From trial & error → to structured execution
    Claude does not just generate code, it plans, validates, and executes step by step.
  • From fragmented tools → to one continuous flow
    Design, implementation, testing, and packaging all happen in a single environment.
  • From idea → to deployable artifact in one session
    The result is not a prototype, but something you can actually use immediately.

What makes it “cool”

The real “wow” factor is the combination of speed, structure, and completeness.

In a very short time, you go from:

“I want a custom animated chart”

to:

A working, deployable SAP Analytics Cloud Custom Widget with real data and animation

And you do this without setting up complex tooling, without manually wiring every piece together, and without leaving your terminal.

It feels less like coding and more like directing a build process.

entry.title: Re: Migrating SAP PI/PO XI Proxy scenarios to Integration Suite – Key considerations and recommendat
entry.content:
Hi @Dhinesh_Kumar1m8 ,

In design time, the consequence of re-pointing destination "SAP_PROXY_ESR" from SAP PI/PO to Integration Suite, is that new development / changes in ESR cannot be done. Integration Suite would become the place for such new development / interface modifications.

In runtime, separate destinations can be maintained, pointing to 1) AAE and 2) CPI pipeline common entry point for XI iflow. Finally, we can control which interfaces should use which destination from SXMSIF and SXMB_ADM.

Hope this is helpful.

entry.title: Week 2 – Building AI Agents with CrewAI and Generative AI Hub
entry.content:
This exercise introduces AI agent development by combining CrewAI (an agent framework) and SAP's Generative AI Hub as the LLM provider to create intelligent, task-oriented agents.

Welcome to week 2 of the April Developer Challenge on AI! Last week you learned how to build a RAG use case with the grounding service and the orchestration service. This week you'll build an AI agent using CrewAI, and Generative AI Hub.

What You'll Achieve

By working through this exercise, you'll learn how to:

  • Build Your First AI Agent: Create an intelligent agent with a specific role, goal, and backstory using the CrewAI framework
  • Define Agent Tasks: Structure concrete objectives and expected outputs that guide agent behavior
  • Integrate Multiple Technologies: Connect CrewAI with LLMs via SAP Generative AI Hub
  • Process Complex Queries: Enable agents to analyze user input, make decisions, and provide structured recommendations

The exercise implements a Hamburg Social Welfare Case Manager agent that can process citizen inquiries, assess eligibility for welfare programs, and generate personalized support plans. This demonstrates how AI agents can be designed to handle domain-specific scenarios requiring structured analysis and decision-making capabilities.

What is CrewAI?

CrewAI is a third-party open-source Python library. As the name suggests, you can use it to build a crew of agents that have a set of tools available to accomplish certain tasks. CrewAI uses tasks to bridge the gap between high-level goals and concrete agent actions, assigning specific objectives and expected outputs. For now, your agent will only respond to incoming queries.

Implementation Components

Agent Configuration

The Hamburg Social Welfare Case Manager agent is configured with:

  • Role: Hamburg Social Welfare Case Manager
  • Goal: Process and manage social welfare applications, matching citizens with appropriate services
  • Backstory: Experienced case manager with deep knowledge of Hamburg's social services
  • LLM: Uses sap/gpt-4o from Generative AI Hub
  • Verbose Mode: Enabled for detailed execution tracking

Task Definition

Tasks in CrewAI define:

  • Description: Specific instructions for processing user inquiries
  • Expected Output: Structured assessments with eligibility determination and support plans
  • Agent Assignment: Links the task to the welfare agent

Interactive Workflow

The system includes:

  • User input prompt for welfare inquiries
  • Dynamic task creation based on user questions
  • Crew execution with verbose output
  • Formatted assessment report generation

Testing Your Agent

Try asking various social welfare questions to test the agent's capabilities in:

  • Analyzing citizen circumstances and needs
  • Determining eligibility for welfare programs
  • Recommending local services and resources
  • Creating personalized support plans

Additional Resources

  • Challenge Blog Post
  • Week 1 Challenge - INCLUDING INFO ON HOW TO GET STARTED!!
  • Example Repo
  • CodeJam on code-based agents

To participate: Submit a screenshot of your last cell including output as a comment below! 

You can find the week 2 exercises in the same repo/workbook from last week in the file week2_agent.ipynb 🙂

Kindly suggest, how automatic Purchase requisition can be created for newly added components in production order for consumption which have not been not included existing BOM.

entry.title: in sap analytics cloud story how to lookup a value from previous row
entry.content:
Hello Friends

I want to be able to create a shipment report where yesterday's unshipped (Carryover) deliveries show as Opening Balance of the next day.  I can easily do this in BOBJ (see below) using PREVIOUS function.  Since PREVIOUS is not available in SAC, is there a trick to produce similar report?
